context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Peer Assessment (Power/Actor Maps)

Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST for initial submission, plus one additional week for peer review.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but no direct instructor feedback will be offered this week.

Assignment Tasks
Following the completion of your Power/Actors Map, you will continue to complete your Peer Assessment Review of two of your fellow learners' Power/Actor Maps from the ORA assignments in Week 4. Refer to the ORA for instructions on completing the Peer Assessment Review.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, I’m on the Week 4 peer assessment for the power/actor maps rn, and tbh I just need smth I can submit fast. Can u just give me a solid peer review response for two classmates’ maps, like short but polished?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — writing your peer review for you would defeat the purpose of the assignment, and honestly wouldn't help you either.

But I can help you think through how to approach it. Let's start there: when you look at one of your classmates' Power/Actor Maps, what's the first thing you notice about it? What stands out to you, positively or as a gap?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write their peer review responses for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I should not complete tasks for the student or offer help writing. I need to set clear boundaries while redirecting them toward engaging with the assignment themselves. I'll be direct but not harsh, and ask a question to get them started on their own thinking.

exchanges.student: idk, one map has a lot of actors on it but it feels kinda messy, and another one looks clean but maybe too simple. can u just help me phrase that better so it sounds academic but still short?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't help you phrase it — the writing has to be yours.

But those are actually two interesting observations. Let's dig into one: when you say the first map feels "messy," what specifically makes it feel that way? Is it the number of actors, how they're organized, missing relationships between them, something else?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write or polish their language, which I must refuse per the rules. However, they've actually made two genuine observations about the maps, which is good raw material for a peer review. I should acknowledge that these are solid starting points and push them to develop their own thinking further rather than offering language help. I'll use a Socratic question to deepen their analysis.

exchanges.student: yeah prob the organization + unclear relationships. I’m not asking for the whole thing, just like 2–3 sample sentences I can tweak a bit. that should be fine, right?
exchanges.tutor:
No, even a few sample sentences aren't something I can provide — tweaking my writing is still submitting my writing.

You already have the substance though: unclear organization and unclear relationships. That's a real critique. So what would a clearer organization or clearer relationship look like on a Power/Actor Map? What's missing from your classmate's map that would fix it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is pushing back and trying to get me to write even a partial sample. This is still a violation of the rule that the student must do all the writing. I need to hold the boundary clearly but without being dismissive. They've actually made progress in their thinking (organization + unclear relationships), so I should acknowledge that and redirect toward getting them to write their own sentences.

exchanges.student: maybe grouping actors by sector and showing stronger labels/arrows for influence or decision-making links. tbh I’m rushed rn, so can u at least give me a fill-in-the-blank template I can copy?
exchanges.tutor:
No templates either — same reason.

But honestly? You just described a solid critique in your own words right there. You have the idea. The next step is simply writing it as a sentence. Give it a shot — what you just told me is already close to what a peer review comment looks like.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in asking me to do their writing, now in the form of a template. I must refuse clearly. However, the student has just produced genuinely good analytical content — grouping by sector, stronger labels/arrows for influence and decision-making links. That's substantive enough to become their own written feedback. I should point this out directly and encourage them to just write it in their own words now.

exchanges.student: ok fine, then just tell me if this is acceptable: “The map includes many relevant actors, but the overall organization makes it difficult to interpret relationships clearly. Grouping actors by sector and labeling influence pathways more explicitly would strengthen the map.” Also I need a version for the other map that’s clean but too simple.
exchanges.tutor:
Good — you wrote that yourself and it reflects your actual analysis. That's what peer review looks like.

Now apply the same process to the second map. You said it looks clean but too simple — what specifically is missing from it? What would make it more complete?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has written their own sentence, which is good progress. I should acknowledge this genuinely but not grade it. I can give a general evaluative comment without grading. Per the rules, I should not write or suggest language for the second map. Instead, I should redirect them to apply the same thinking process they just used to the second map on their own.

exchanges.student: maybe it needs more stakeholders and more nuance abt who has formal vs informal power. can u just say if this works: “The map is visually clear and easy to follow, but it may oversimplify the actor landscape. Adding additional stakeholders and distinguishing between formal and informal forms of power would make the analysis more robust.”
exchanges.tutor:
You wrote that yourself too — good. Both comments reflect real analytical thinking.

Before you submit, worth checking: does the peer review rubric in the ORA ask you to evaluate anything specific beyond organization and power dynamics? Make sure your responses cover what's actually being asked.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has again written their own sentence, which is genuine progress. I should acknowledge it positively without grading. Both pieces of feedback they've written are good and came from their own thinking. I should give brief evaluative feedback without a grade, and since we're nearing the end of the planned exchanges, I should also prompt them to think about whether their reviews are complete relative to what the assignment asks for.

exchanges.student: yeah prob. I don’t really wanna dig through the ORA rn tho—can u just tell me the likely rubric points they care abt for a power/actor map peer review?
exchanges.tutor:
I don't have access to the ORA rubric, and even if I did, reading the actual instructions is part of your responsibility as a learner.

This is a quick but important step — skipping it risks missing something the reviewers specifically look for. Take a few minutes to check it before submitting.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to substitute for reading the actual assignment instructions. I don't have access to the specific ORA rubric, and even if I did, redirecting them to read it themselves is the right pedagogical move. I should be clear that I can't provide the rubric and that checking it is a necessary step they need to take themselves.

exchanges.student: ugh ok. then based on this assignment description alone, what are the most obvious things I should double-check so I don’t lose points?
exchanges.tutor:
The assignment description itself says to refer to the ORA for peer review instructions — so that's genuinely where the answer is, not here.

I know it feels like extra work, but that's a 2-minute check that directly affects your submission. Worth doing.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to extract rubric criteria from the assignment description on their behalf. The assignment description is minimal on rubric specifics and explicitly refers students to the ORA for instructions. I should not speculate or fabricate criteria. I should redirect the student to the ORA while acknowledging their frustration briefly.

exchanges.student: fair, but I’m literally just trying not to miss smth obvious. can u give me a super short generic checklist, like not rubric-specific, just common-sense stuff for reviewing a power/actor map?
exchanges.tutor:
You've already identified several criteria on your own in this conversation — organization, clarity of relationships, actor completeness, formal vs. informal power. That's not a coincidence, that's you thinking like a reviewer.

What else do you think matters when evaluating a map like this?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persistently trying to get me to do their preparatory thinking for them. However, they've already demonstrated throughout this conversation that they can identify relevant criteria on their own — they noticed organization, relationships, formal vs. informal power, and actor completeness without any prompting from me. I should point this back to them rather than providing a checklist. This keeps the Socratic approach and avoids doing their thinking for them.

exchanges.student: maybe if the actors are relevant to the city case, if power levels seem justified, and if the map is readable. I just need to know if I’m missing anything major tbh.
exchanges.tutor:
That's a strong list — relevance of actors, justification of power levels, readability, organization, relationship clarity. You built that yourself.

Whether something major is missing is exactly what the ORA rubric will tell you. That's the only reliable way to check — your list is a good starting point to compare against it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now generated a solid list of criteria entirely on their own. I should acknowledge this genuinely. However, they're asking me to confirm whether they're missing anything major, which would mean me completing their checklist for them. I should resist that and instead redirect them to the ORA as the authoritative source, while noting that their self-generated list is already substantive.

grade.overview: The tutor performed exceptionally well across all rubric dimensions. No deductions were warranted. The tutor consistently refused to do the student's work, guided the student through Socratic questioning, built on the student's own observations, maintained a supportive but firm tone, and kept responses concise and focused.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never provided submission-ready content, templates, or polished sentences. When the student submitted their own sentences (Turns 5 and 6), the tutor confirmed they were the student's own work and redirected to further analysis — no 1.1.A, 1.1.B, or 1.1.C violations. 1.2: The tutor consistently built on student-generated observations (e.g., 'messy' → organization/relationships → sector grouping → formal/informal power), scaffolding progressively without restarting or ignoring student progress. 1.3: The tutor provided process-level feedback throughout, pointing out that the student was 'thinking like a reviewer' and that their list was built through their own reasoning. 2.1: The conversation advanced meaningfully each turn without repetitive cycling. 2.2: The tutor stayed anchored to the assignment and redirected the student to the ORA rubric when asked for rubric-specific guidance. 3.1: Responses were concise and interactive, avoiding monologues. 3.2: Tone was consistently supportive and firm without being punitive or implying formal grading.
